anchor_material.question: United 300 and 300 were both made in what country?

delta_p.trigger: Apply when the question (or summary_1) lists two or more short title-like tokens (for example: 'X and Y ...') or when summary_1 explicitly notes a missing link for a short/ambiguous title such as 'United 300' and '300'.
delta_p.preserve_rule: Keep all existing base-prompt requirements: produce exactly one compact single-line retrieval query string, anchor the query to visible tokens from summary_1, do not add explanatory text or invent facts, and preserve the single-field output contract.
delta_p.change_rule: When triggered, form the single query by concatenating the visible short title tokens from the inputs (in the same order they appear in the question/summary) and append the entertainment disambiguator 'film' plus a locality keyword to bias toward production/manufacture location. Use the minimal token set: '<visible_title1> <visible_title2> film country'. Do not add additional words, punctuation, or full sentences.
delta_p.drop_or_avoid_rule: Do not append unrelated domain disambiguators, do not assert any country or other facts, do not include multi-word quoted passages from target texts, and do not output anything other than the single query string. Only add the 'film' disambiguator when multiple title-like tokens are present (per the disambiguation rule).
delta_p.output_contract: Emit exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The string should be composed only of visible title tokens from the inputs plus the two keywords 'film' and 'country' as specified.
delta_p.why_local_transition: The change nudges the agent to retrieve the correct disambiguation/film pages by explicitly treating the short tokens as film titles (allowed because the question lists multiple titles) and by adding 'country' to focus results on production location, without changing any other base behavior.

candidate_materials.question: What year was the crime novel written by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle written, featuring Sherlock Holmes?

delta_p.trigger: If summary_1 mentions more than one Sherlock Holmes novel title (multiple distinct title anchors from Doyle) AND the question text contains the token "crime" or the phrase "crime novel"
delta_p.preserve_rule: Keep the original single-line, single-string query contract and the instruction to use visible anchors from summary_1; do not add explanatory text, punctuation beyond the query tokens, or extra fields.
delta_p.change_rule: When triggered, do not reuse the same title token already used in the current query. Instead, replace the named title in your query with the other Sherlock Holmes novel title that appears in summary_1 (for example, if your query used 'The Sign of the Four', switch the title token to the other candidate shown in summary_1 such as 'The Hound of the Baskervilles'), and keep the remainder of the query focused on publication/writing year and the author (e.g., retain tokens like publication year and Sir Arthur Conan Doyle). Compose a compact keyword-style search reflecting that swap.
delta_p.drop_or_avoid_rule: Do not add full sentences, justification, or the downstream answer; avoid introducing entertainment disambiguators unless the question explicitly required one; do not change other relation keywords (publication year, Sir Arthur Conan Doyle) or add unrelated qualifiers.
delta_p.output_contract: Emit exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) that uses the alternative title anchor plus concise relation keywords (e.g., publication year Sir Arthur Conan Doyle) and nothing else.
delta_p.why_local_transition: The first-hop output targeted one candidate title but the question's phrasing ('crime novel') and summary_1 show multiple Doyle novel anchors; this small behavioral switch forces a second-hop query that targets the other candidate novel present in the visible inputs so the pipeline can retrieve its publication year.

delta_p.trigger: If summary_1 names a specific Doyle Sherlock Holmes novel (for example, 'The Hound of the Baskervilles') while the question refers to that work with an ambiguous phrase like "the crime novel", adjust the retrieval focus to surface not only publication details but also named inspirations, local legends, or real‑life models associated with that novel.
delta_p.preserve_rule: Keep the existing contract and style: emit exactly one compact, keyword-style single-line query anchored to visible tokens from summary_1; prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords and do not add explanatory text or full-sentence phrasing.
delta_p.change_rule: When the trigger applies, produce a single query that combines the visible novel title with a short relation keyword aimed at uncovering origin/inspiration information. Use one of these concise relation tokens after the title: inspiration | inspired by | based on legend | real-life model | origin. Example form: <Novel Title> inspiration. Do not add additional clauses, punctuation, or the full user question.
delta_p.drop_or_avoid_rule: Do not invent or insert any specific person or hidden-support names not present in the visible inputs. Do not append the full user question or natural-language phrases; avoid extra stopwords, punctuation, or multi-clause queries. Do not broaden to unrelated keywords (films, adaptations) unless those are already required by the disambiguation rules.
delta_p.output_contract: Emit exactly one single-line, keyword-style query string and no other fields or commentary. The query must use only anchors visible in summary_1 plus one short relation token from the change_rule list.
delta_p.why_local_transition: This is the smallest focused change that redirects a publication-year query toward retrieval of named inspirations/legends tied to the same novel. It preserves the original anchoring behavior while adding one compact relation token to surface the missing local figure without inventing names or breaking the single-query contract.

request.rendered_prompt:
You are selecting the local prompt-feedback update that is semantically closest to a fixed anchor update.

Each material contains feedback for the HotpotQA second-hop query generator.
Compare the anchor against all candidate materials and select exactly one candidate whose feedback represents the most similar query-generation behavior update.

Here, semantic distance means how differently the retrieval query behavior would need to change:
- which entity, title, alias, location, class, or task anchors to preserve, add, remove, or disambiguate
- whether an anchor is grounded in the visible inputs, misleading, or an unsupported hypothesized answer
- which bridge relation, comparison relation, role, property, or information need to emphasize
- which evidence family, domain, or answer type the query should target
- which distractors, wrong senses, prior answers, or noisy entity families to avoid
- whether the candidate scope should be broadened, narrowed, preserved, or re-centered
- whether the query should use compact keywords, preserve a comparison, or restate an open information need
- what retrieval failure the feedback is intended to correct

Important:
- Judge the semantic update represented by the feedback itself.
- Do not judge which update is more correct or likely to improve retrieval.
- Do not select a candidate merely because it shares entities, topics, question wording, or query tokens with the anchor.
- Do not select a candidate merely because its feedback is longer or more detailed.
- Source-style details such as infoboxes, lead sentences, cast sections, tables, or list parsing are secondary unless they change the retrieval target.
- Broadening from one entity to multiple comparison subjects is different from narrowing a broad query to one missing entity.
- Removing a misleading visible anchor is different from preventing insertion of an unsupported candidate.
- Changing from a verbose sentence to compact keywords matters only when it changes the retrieval strategy or target.
- If a material contains multiple delta_p edges, compare the complete ordered trace and its net retrieval-policy change, not only matching individual edges.
- Select exactly one candidate. If candidates request materially equivalent updates, choose the one with the smallest sample_index.

Return JSON only:
{
  "selected_sample_index": 0,
  "shared_update_pattern": "short description of the retrieval-policy update shared by the anchor and selected candidate",
  "rationale": "short explanation of why this candidate is semantically closest to the anchor",
  "confidence": 0.0
}

Requirements:
- selected_sample_index must exactly match one candidate sample_index.
- confidence must be between 0.0 and 1.0.
- Do not return markdown or additional keys.

Agent:
query
